user: i have uncommitted changes and need to switch branch urgently
assistant: Accepted. `git stash` — saves your changes, cleans the working tree. Then switch branches. When you come back: `git stash pop` to restore. If you have multiple stashes: `git stash list` to see them, `git stash pop stash@{1}` to pick one. Warning: stash doesn't save untracked files by default. Add `-u` flag if you have new files: `git stash -u`.

user: when should i use merge vs rebase
assistant: Accepted. Rule of thumb: rebase for your own feature branches, merge for integrating into main. Rebase rewrites commit history — your commits land on top of the target branch cleanly. Good for keeping history readable. Merge preserves history exactly as it happened. Good for shared branches where rewriting history would break everyone else's work. Never rebase commits that exist on a remote shared branch. That's the rule most people violate.

user: i ran git reset --hard and lost my work
assistant: Hold on. Don't do anything else. `git reflog` — run it now. Git keeps a local log of every HEAD movement for 90 days by default. Find the commit hash before your reset — it'll look like `abc1234 HEAD@{3}: commit: your message`. Then: `git checkout abc1234` to inspect, or `git reset --hard abc1234` to restore. Works unless you've already garbage-collected or it was an untracked file (those are gone). Paste your reflog output if you need help reading it.

user: how to see a nice git history
assistant: Accepted. `git log --oneline --graph --all` — one line per commit, ASCII branch graph, shows all branches. If you want it as an alias: `git config --global alias.tree 'log --oneline --graph --all --decorate'`, then `git tree` works forever. For more detail per commit: `git log --stat` shows which files changed. For a specific file's history: `git log --follow -p -- path/to/file`.

user: my .env file got committed to git how do i fix it
assistant: Accepted. Fast. Three steps: 1) `git rm --cached .env` — removes from tracking, keeps local file. 2) Add `.env` to `.gitignore`. 3) Commit: `git commit -m 'remove .env from tracking'`. Important: the file is still in your git history. If this repo is public or the .env had real credentials, those are compromised — rotate them immediately regardless. To scrub from history entirely you need `git filter-branch` or `git-filter-repo`, which is a bigger operation.

user: how do i undo my last commit but keep the changes
assistant: Reset with the soft flag pointing one commit back. That undoes the commit but leaves your changes staged, ready to recommit differently. If you want the changes unstaged too, use a mixed reset instead of soft. Never use a hard reset for this unless you actually want to lose the changes.

user: whats git commit amend actually for
assistant: Rewrites your last commit instead of creating a new one — use it to fix a typo in the commit message, or add a forgotten file to the commit you just made. Only amend commits you haven't pushed yet, amending a pushed commit rewrites history others may have already pulled.

user: whats the actual difference between git fetch and git pull
assistant: Fetch downloads the latest changes from remote but doesn't touch your working files, it just updates your local view of the remote branch. Pull is fetch plus merge, or rebase, into your current branch immediately. Fetch first if you want to review what changed before merging it in.

user: any tips for writing better commit messages
assistant: First line under 50 characters, imperative mood, 'Fix login bug' not 'Fixed' or 'Fixing'. That first line should complete the sentence 'If applied, this commit will'. If the change needs more explanation, leave a blank line then add detail in the body. Future you searching git log will thank present you.

user: how do i squash a bunch of messy commits into one before merging
assistant: Start an interactive rebase covering however many recent commits you want to combine. It opens an editor listing them, change pick to squash on all but the first one, save, then edit the combined commit message. Only do this on commits that aren't shared with anyone else yet.

user: when would i actually use git cherry-pick
assistant: When you need one specific commit from another branch without merging the whole branch — like a hotfix that landed on a feature branch but also needs to go straight to main. Cherry-pick applies just that one commit onto your current branch. Useful, but overusing it fragments history, a real merge is usually cleaner.

user: stuck in a merge conflict and dont know how to resolve it properly
assistant: Accepted. Open the conflicted file, git marks the conflict with markers showing your version and theirs. Decide what the correct final version is, delete the markers, save. Then stage the resolved file and commit to finish the merge. Talk to whoever wrote the other side if you're not sure which version is actually correct.

user: added a file to gitignore but git is still tracking changes to it whats wrong
assistant: Gitignore only stops NEW untracked files from being added, it doesn't untrack a file that's already committed. Remove it from tracking while keeping it on disk, commit that removal, and the gitignore rule will take effect from then on.

user: how do i rename a git branch, i typod the name
assistant: If it's your current branch, rename it locally with the branch move command. If it's already pushed, also delete the old remote branch and push the new one. Update anyone else's local clones so they don't keep pushing to the old name.

user: whats git blame actually useful for besides blaming people
assistant: Shows who last changed each line and in which commit, great for understanding why code is the way it is. Find the weird line, blame it, get the commit hash, then look at the full commit for context. Usually more about context than blame.

user: how do i tag a release in git
assistant: Create an annotated tag with a message describing the release. Push it separately, tags don't push automatically with regular commits. Annotated tags are preferred over lightweight ones since they store the tagger, date, and message.

user: whats the difference between origin and upstream when working with a forked repo
assistant: Origin is your fork, where you push your own changes. Upstream is the original repo you forked from, you add it as a second remote so you can pull in their latest changes without it interfering with your own fork's history. Standard flow: fetch from upstream, merge into your main, push to origin.

user: is force pushing ever actually ok or should i just never do it
assistant: It's fine on your own feature branch that nobody else has pulled from, say after a rebase. It's dangerous on a shared branch like main, since it overwrites history others have already based work on, silently dropping their commits from the remote. If you must force push a shared branch, use the safer force-with-lease option instead of a plain force, it fails safely if someone else pushed in the meantime.

user: how does git bisect actually work for finding which commit broke something
assistant: You tell it a known-good commit and the current known-bad one, git checks out the midpoint, you test and tell it good or bad, it keeps halving the range until it lands on the exact commit that introduced the bug. Binary search, applied to your commit history.

user: git says im in detached HEAD state is my work in danger
assistant: Not in danger yet, but be careful. It means you're not on a branch, just looking at a specific commit directly — any new commits you make here won't belong to any branch and can get lost once you switch away. If you want to keep work made here, create a branch right now before doing anything else. Then it's safe.
